1004	  infrastructure and the dimension-shift step proved above. Cut the resolution into
1005	  its cosyzygy short exact sequences \((S_m) : 0 \to Z^m \to J^m \to Z^{m+1} \to 0\)
1006	  with \(Z^0 = A\) and acyclic middle terms, as furnished by
1007	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}.

1010	  \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) \cong G(A)\), and by \(R^0 G \cong G\)
1011	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) the right-hand side is \((R^0 G)(A)\).
1012	  This is the case \(n = 0\).
1013

1015	  \((S_0), \ldots, (S_{n-2})\) all have acyclic middle terms, so the dimension-shift
1016	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} applied to them gives, for each \(k \geq 1\),
1017	  isomorphisms \((R^k G)(Z^{m+1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(Z^m)\) for \(0 \le m \le n-2\)

1019	  \[
1020	    (R^n G)(A)
1021	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
1022	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)

22	\section{Setup and motivation}
23	
24	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
25	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
26	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
27	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
28	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
29	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
30	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
31	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
32	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
33	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
34	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
35	
36	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
37	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
38	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
39	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
40	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
41	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
42	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
43	\[
44	  \begin{array}{ccc}
45	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
46	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
47	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
48	  \end{array}
49	\]
50	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
51	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
52	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
53	
54	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
55	
56	\begin{definition}
57	
58	\leanok
59	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
60	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
62	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
63	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
64	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
65	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
66	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
67	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
68	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
69	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
70	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
71	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
72	  % the unit ideal, see
73	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
74	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
75	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
76	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
77	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
78	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
79	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
80	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
81	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
82	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
83	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
84	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
85	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
86	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
87	  \[
88	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
89	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
90	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
91	  \]
92	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
93	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
94	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
95	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
96	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
97	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
98	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
99	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
100	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
101	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
102	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
103	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
104	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
105	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).

172	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
173	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
174	
175	It is convenient to factor the construction of
176	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
177	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
178	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
179	
180	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
181	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
182	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
183	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
184	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
185	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
186	\[
187	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
188	    \;=\;
189	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
190	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
191	\]
192	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
193	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
194	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
195	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
196	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
197	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
198	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
199	
200	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
201	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
202	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
203	\[
204	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
205	  \qquad
206	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
207	\]
208	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
209	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
210	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
211	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
212	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
213	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
214	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
215	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
216	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
217	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
218	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
219	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
220	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
221	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
222	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
223	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
224	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
225	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
226	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
227	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
228	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
229	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
230	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
231	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
232	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
233	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
234	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
235	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
236	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
237	
238	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
239	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
240	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
241	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
242	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
243	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
244	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
245	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
246	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
247	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
248	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
249	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
250	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
251	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
252	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
253	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
254	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
255	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
256	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
257	
258	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
259	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
260	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
261	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
262	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
263	
264	\begin{definition}\leanok
265	[Arrow of an affine cover]
266	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
267	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
268	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
269	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
270	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
271	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
272	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
273	  apply.
274	\end{definition}
275	
276	\begin{definition}[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
277	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
278	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOver, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOverAug}
279	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
280	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
281	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
282	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
283	  \[
284	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
285	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
286	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
287	  \]
288	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
289	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
290	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
291	\end{definition}

293	\begin{definition}\leanok
294	[Push--pull object map]
295	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
296	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
297	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
298	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
299	  \[
300	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
301	  \]
302	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
303	\end{definition}
304	
305	\begin{definition}[Push--pull comparison map]
306	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_eq_raw, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self_gen, AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardComp_hom_app_id, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_comp}
308	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
309	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
310	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
311	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
312	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
313	  \[
314	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
315	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
316	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
317	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
318	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
319	      \;=\;
320	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
321	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
322	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
323	  \]
324	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
325	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
326	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
327	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
328	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
329	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
330	\end{definition}

332	\begin{lemma}
333	
334	\leanok
335	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
336	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
337	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
338	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
339	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
340	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
341	\end{lemma}
342	
343	\begin{proof}
344	
345	\leanok
346	  A direct calculation with comparison isomorphisms, with no sectionwise content.
347	  Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward comparison as the
348	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison; the identity leg then collapses
349	  by the identity normalisation of the pseudofunctor together with right unitality,
350	  and the two over-triangle transport coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
351	\end{proof}

353	\begin{lemma}
354	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
355	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
356	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_pentagon}
357	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
358	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
359	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
360	  \[
361	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
362	  \]
363	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
364	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
365	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
366	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
367	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
368	\end{lemma}

371	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
372	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
373	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
374	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of

376	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
377	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
378	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
379	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
380	\end{proof}

416	\begin{lemma}
417	
418	\leanok
419	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
420	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
421	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}
422	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
423	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the
424	  over-triangle \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\), and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
425	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
426	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
427	  collapses to the transport-light form
428	  \[
429	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
430	      \;\circ\; e_2
431	    \;=\;
432	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
433	      \;\circ\; e_3,
434	  \]
435	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by
436	  \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\). It states the over-triangle cancellation in the
437	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} with the
438	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
439	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
440	  composition (pentagon) law.
441	\end{lemma}
442	
443	\begin{proof}
444	
445	\leanok
446	  Substitute the over-triangle equality \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\) to replace
447	  transport coercions by identities, then verify the cancellation by direct
448	  calculation with the pushforward comparison.
449	\end{proof}

504	\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}
505	
506	\begin{definition}
507	[Standard affine cover from a spanning family]
508	  \label{def:standard_affine_cover}
509	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}
510	  \mathlibok
511	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Cover.Open}).}
512	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring (so \(R = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R,
513	  \mathcal{O})\)) and let \(s : \iota \to R\) be a family of elements that generate
514	  the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\). The
515	  \emph{standard affine open cover} of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) associated with
516	  \(s\) is the affine open cover whose index set is \(\iota\), whose \(i\)-th member
517	  is the basic open \(D(s_i)\), and whose \(i\)-th structure morphism is the map of
518	  affine schemes induced by the localisation homomorphism \(R \to R_{s_i}\); that
519	  is, in Mathlib notation the defining equation
520	  \(\bigl(\text{affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}\bigr)_f\, i =
521	  \operatorname{Spec.map}\bigl(\text{algebraMap}\; R\; (\operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i))\bigr)\),
522	  so that the sections over the \(i\)-th piece are the \emph{away} localisation
523	  \(R_{s_i} = \operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i)\) and the geometric piece is
524	  \(D(s_i)\). This is exactly the standard open covering
525	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_i D(s_i)\) by basic opens, packaged as a single
526	  cover object. It is the spanning-family bundle \((s, hs)\) that the affine
527	  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is stated over.
528	\end{definition}

530	\subsection{The categorical--module bridge (L1)}
531	\label{sec:cech_l1_bridge}
532	
533	The affine vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is a statement about the
534	\emph{section} {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
535	\mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- a cochain complex of
536	abelian groups --- whereas the combinatorial contracting-homotopy core operates on a
537	concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules. The passage between the two, the
538	\emph{categorical--module bridge}, decomposes into two self-contained statements: the
539	section identification (each section group is an away localisation, with the
540	restriction maps the canonical localisation maps) and the homology-to-exactness
541	reduction (vanishing of the abstract homology is equivalent to exactness of the
542	underlying localised-module complex). These are isolated as
543	Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and
544	Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} below; the assembly --- feeding each
545	positive-degree node through the local-to-global exactness criterion and closing with
546	the dependent combinatorial port --- is carried out in the proof of
547	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.
548	
549	\begin{definition}
550	
551	[Quasi-coherent sections over a basic open are an away localisation]
552	  \label{def:qcoh_sections_localized}
553	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.qcohSectionsAwayLocalized,
554	    AlgebraicGeometry.basicOpen_sprod,
555	    AlgebraicGeometry.iInf_fin_succ,
556	    AlgebraicGeometry.qcohRestriction_eq_comparison,
557	    AlgebraicGeometry.tP,
558	    AlgebraicGeometry.tU,
559	    AlgebraicGeometry.phiL,
560	    AlgebraicGeometry.phi,
561	    AlgebraicGeometry.phi_eq_phiL,
562	    AlgebraicGeometry.restr_bridge}
563	  \uses{def:section_cech_complex, def:standard_affine_cover}
564	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Schemes, Lemma \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
565	  % items (4)--(5) (read from references/stacks-schemes.tex, L701--707).
566	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "(4) For every $f\in R$ we have $\Gamma(D(f), \widetilde M) = M_f$
567	  % as an $R_f$-module.
568	  % (5) Whenever $D(g) \subset D(f)$ the restriction mappings
569	  % on $\mathcal{O}_{\Spec(R)}$ and $\widetilde M$
570	  % are the maps
571	  % $R_f \to R_g$ and $M_f \to M_g$ from Lemma
572	  % \ref{lemma-standard-open}."
573	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, \texttt{lemma-spec-sheaves} (Tag 01HV),
574	  items (4)--(5).}
575	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
576	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\)-module, and write
577	  \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\) for its module of global
578	  sections. For every \(g \in R\), the restriction-of-sections map
579	  \(M = \mathcal{F}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g))\) exhibits the
580	  section group \(\mathcal{F}(D(g))\) as the away localisation \(M_g\) of \(M\); that
581	  is, this map is an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) for the multiplicative
582	  submonoid \(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g\) of powers of \(g\). Moreover, for
583	  \(D(g_2) \subseteq D(g_1)\) the restriction map
584	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(g_1)) \to \mathcal{F}(D(g_2))\) is, under these identifications, the
585	  canonical localisation map \(M_{g_1} \to M_{g_2}\) (the unique \(R\)-linear map
586	  compatible with the localisation units). Item~(4) of Tag~01HV gives the first
587	  assertion for \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) (\(\Gamma(D(g), \widetilde{M}) = M_g\)
588	  as an \(R_g\)-module) and item~(5) gives the second (the restriction along
589	  \(D(g) \subset D(f)\) is the localisation map \(M_f \to M_g\)).
590	
591	  The module-level input on the side of Mathlib is the development
592	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Tilde}: the comparison map
593	  \((\operatorname{tilde.toOpen} M\, (D(g))).\mathrm{hom}\) carries the instance
594	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} g)\), and
595	  \(\operatorname{isUnit\_algebraMap\_end\_basicOpen}\) records that \(g\) acts
596	  invertibly on the sections over \(D(g)\); the restriction compatibility is
597	  \(\operatorname{tilde.toOpen\_res}\). These supply the statement \emph{verbatim} for
598	  the standard sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\). For an \emph{arbitrary} quasi-coherent
599	  \(\mathcal{F}\) the remaining step is the canonical comparison
600	  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})}\), i.e.\
601	  that the counit \(\operatorname{Scheme.Modules.fromTildeΓ}\) is an isomorphism. This
602	  is \emph{not} yet available in Mathlib for a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\):
603	  Mathlib provides it only from a \emph{global} presentation
604	  (\(\operatorname{isIso\_fromTildeΓ\_of\_presentation}\)), whereas quasi-coherence
605	  supplies only \emph{local} presentation data on a cover. Globalising the local data
606	  on the affine \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) --- the affine equivalence
607	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(\operatorname{Spec} R) \simeq \operatorname{Mod}(R)\), Stacks
608	  Tag~01I8 --- is the one genuine gap this definition records as a build target; it is
609	  the canonical missing affine equivalence and is self-contained.
610	
611	  Two combinatorial--geometric ingredients package the identification into the form
612	  consumed by Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}. First, the multi-index
613	  intersection identity
614	  \(\bigl(\bigcap_k D(s_{\sigma(k)})\bigr) = D\bigl(\prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\bigr)\) in
615	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R).\mathrm{Opens}\) (\(\operatorname{basicOpen\_sprod}\), proved
616	  by induction on the index length using the infimum-splitting helper
617	  \(\operatorname{iInf\_fin\_succ}\) and \(\operatorname{PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen\_mul}\)):
618	  it rewrites the \((p+1)\)-fold intersection over a multi-index \(\sigma\) as the single
619	  basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\), so the
620	  section group there is the away localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). Second, the restriction
621	  compatibility of item~(5) is made explicit: for a basic-open inclusion
622	  \(D(b) \subseteq D(a)\) the presheaf restriction map of \(\widetilde{M}\) between the
623	  two section groups \emph{is} the away-localisation comparison
624	  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison}\) of the two localisation units
625	  (\(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\), by the uniqueness
626	  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison\_unique}\) of a map of localised modules);
627	  this is the single differential brick that the {\v C}ech coface match of
628	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} feeds on.
629	
630	  The identification is packaged by a few helpers: the two localised-module endpoints
631	  \(\operatorname{tP}, \operatorname{tU}\) (the section groups
632	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) named as away localisations at the two indices
633	  involved in a face), the per-\(\sigma\) comparison
634	  \(\varphi_\sigma = \operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.iso}\) realised both as the bare
635	  localisation comparison \(\operatorname{phiL}\) and as its sheaf-level avatar
636	  \(\operatorname{phi}\) (their agreement is \(\operatorname{phi\_eq\_phiL}\)), and the
637	  accessor-bridge \(\operatorname{restr\_bridge}\) that exhibits the presheaf
638	  restriction map through these comparisons.
639	\end{definition}

673	  % L58--67).
674	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
675	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
676	  % is identified with the complex
677	  % $$
678	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
679	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
680	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to

683	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
684	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (identification with the
685	  complex of localisations).}
686	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec}
687	  R}\)-module and \(\mathcal{U}\) the standard cover associated with a spanning family
688	  \(s : \iota \to R\). Under the section identification of
689	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}, the section {\v C}ech complex
690	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
691	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} is isomorphic, as a cochain complex, to
692	  the concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules
693	  \[
694	    D^\bullet : \quad
695	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
696	    \prod_{\sigma : \{0,1\} \to \iota} M_{s_\sigma} \;\longrightarrow\;
697	    \cdots,
698	    \qquad
699	    s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)},
700	  \]
701	  whose differential is the alternating sum of the canonical localisation maps. The
702	  intended top-level conclusion is the directed homology vanishing
703	  \[
704	    1 \leq p
705	    \;\Longrightarrow\;
706	    \operatorname{IsZero}\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
707	      \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p\bigr),
708	  \]
709	  which is the form the affine vanishing
710	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} (\(\operatorname{sectionCech\_affine\_vanishing}\))
711	  consumes; it is read off from positive-degree exactness of the underlying group
712	  homomorphisms,
713	  \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}\bigl(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to D^{p+1}\bigr)\), which is
714	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}.
715	
716	  The reduction from the abstract homology to this group-level exactness is \emph{not}
717	  a \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\) statement: the degree-\(p\) object of
718	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) is the \emph{categorical
719	  product}
720	  \(\prod^{c}_{\sigma : \mathrm{Fin}(p+1) \to \iota}
721	  \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) in the category \(\mathrm{Ab}\) of abelian groups (the
722	  alternating-coface-map complex of the \(\mathrm{Ab}\)-valued cosimplicial object
723	  \(\operatorname{sectionCechCosimplicial}\)), not an \(R\)-module object, so the
724	  module-category criterion \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.moduleCat\_exact\_iff}\) does
725	  not apply. The correct chain runs through three steps, isolated as the sub-lemmas
726	  below: \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology is \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) at
727	  \(p\) (\(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\)); \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\)
728	  of a short complex in \(\mathrm{Ab}\) is the underlying-group exactness
729	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\) via the abelian
730	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
731	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}); and the categorical product is moved to the
732	  per-\(\sigma\) localised modules by the element-level product equivalence
733	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
734	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\)
735	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}), under which the {\v C}ech coface
736	  differential matches the localisation differential \(\operatorname{dDiff}\) of
737	  \(D^\bullet\) (the abstract cosimplicial unfold
738	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply} followed by the tilde-bridge identification
739	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). Transporting the
740	  already-proved exactness \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\) of
741	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact} across the product equivalence then
742	  discharges the group-level exactness. The active target is the tilde case
743	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\), for which the per-coordinate comparisons are
744	  immediate from \(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\); a general
745	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) is reduced to this case via the affine equivalence
746	  \(\mathcal{F} \cong \widetilde{\Gamma\mathcal{F}}\) (Stacks Tag~01I8), the
747	  separately-deferred globalisation gap recorded in
748	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}.
749	\end{lemma}
750	
751	\begin{proof}
752	  \uses{def:qcoh_sections_localized, def:section_cech_complex}
753	  The term identification is the degreewise statement of
754	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: the degree-\(p\) section group
755	  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is \(\prod_\sigma M_{s_\sigma}\), since
756	  each \((p+1)\)-fold intersection \(D(s_{\sigma(0)}) \cap \cdots \cap
757	  D(s_{\sigma(p)})\) is the basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product
758	  \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\) and the sections there are the away
759	  localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). The differential identification is item~(5) of
760	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: each face restriction
761	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})) \to \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is the
762	  canonical localisation map \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\), so the
763	  alternating sum of restrictions is the alternating sum of localisation maps, the
764	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) (the coface match of
765	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). The homology vanishing then follows in
766	  three steps. First, \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\) turns
767	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology at \(p\) into \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) of
768	  the short complex at \(p\). Second, because the degree-\(p\) objects are
769	  \emph{categorical products in \(\mathrm{Ab}\)} rather than \(R\)-modules, the abelian
770	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
771	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}) --- not the module-category criterion ---
772	  reduces \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) to the underlying-group exactness
773	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\). Third, the
774	  element-level product equivalence
775	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
776	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
777	  identifies each product group with the per-\(\sigma\) localised module \(D^p\), so the
778	  group-level exactness is exactly \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to
779	  D^{p+1})\), supplied by \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\)
780	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}).
781	
782	  The identification of the abstract section complex with the concrete localised
783	  complex \(D^\bullet\) --- together with the construction of the prepend transport
784	  \(c\) and the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\) discharging the unit, shift, and
785	  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\)/\(\mathtt{hsh}\)/\(\mathtt{hcomm}\)
786	  of the dependent combinatorial port --- is the bulk of the bridge. The non-circular
787	  route is reflection along the localisation equivalence on its essential image (the
788	  global-sections functor \(\Gamma\) is the inverse equivalence there, so it reflects
789	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of homology); it is \emph{not} the assertion that
790	  ``\(\Gamma\) preserves homology'', which would presuppose the very affine exactness
791	  being proved.
792	\end{proof}

967	  \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\): the former by
968	  \(\operatorname{qcohSectionsAwayLocalized}\) together with the multi-index
969	  intersection identity \(\operatorname{basicOpen\_sprod}\) (which rewrites
970	  \(\bigcap_k D(s_{\sigma(k)})\) as the single basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\)), the latter
971	  by construction. Hence there is a canonical \(R\)-linear isomorphism
972	  \[
973	    \varphi_\sigma :
974	      \operatorname{ToType}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\bigr)
975	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
976	      M_{s_\sigma},
977	  \]
978	  the comparison of the two \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) structures, unique by
979	  \(\operatorname{AwayComparison.comparison\_unique}\) and supplied by
980	  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.iso}\). The naturality square
981	  \[
982	    \varphi_\sigma \;\circ\; \operatorname{sectionCechFaceRestr}(\sigma, i)
983	      \;=\;
984	      \operatorname{dCoface} \;\circ\; \varphi_{\sigma \circ d_i}
985	  \]
986	  is exactly \(\operatorname{qcohRestriction\_eq\_comparison}\) of
987	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: the presheaf restriction of
988	  \(\widetilde{M}\) between the two section groups is the away-localisation comparison
989	  of the two localisation units. This face-naturality is recorded in two forms: at the
990	  bare localisation level as \(\operatorname{phiL\_naturality}\) and at the sheaf level
991	  (with \(\varphi_\sigma = \operatorname{phi}\)) as \(\operatorname{phi\_naturality}\).
992	  Substituting (ii) into (i) and summing with signs
993	  identifies the section {\v C}ech differential with \(\operatorname{dDiff}\).
994	
995	  The isomorphism \(\varphi_\sigma\) is obtained by matching the two descriptions of
996	  the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over \(D(s_\sigma)\): the cosimplicial functor
997	  provides one description and the localisation property of
998	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} provides the other. Their agreement,
999	  together with the naturality square, is the content of this bridge.
1000	\end{proof}

1002	\begin{lemma}
1003	[Abelian-group exactness criterion for the section {\v C}ech complex]
1004	  \label{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}

1006	    AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCech_isZero_homology_of_objD_exact,
1007	    AlgebraicGeometry.ab_hom_finsetSum_apply,

1012	    lem:section_cech_product_equiv, lem:section_cech_coface_match,
1013	    lem:section_cech_module_exact}
1014	  For \(1 \leq p\) the homology of the section {\v C}ech complex
1015	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
1016	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} vanishes,
1017	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
1018	  \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{homology}\, p)\). The argument splits into a homological
1019	  reduction and a ladder transport:
1020	  \begin{itemize}
1021	    \item \emph{Reduction.} By standard homological algebra, vanishing of the
1022	      homology at degree \(q+1\) is equivalent to exactness of the short complex
1023	      around that node, and hence to function exactness of the two consecutive
1024	      coface differentials \(d^q\), \(d^{q+1}\) as maps of abelian groups.
1025	    \item \emph{Ladder transport.} The degreewise additive isomorphism
1026	      (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}) between the section complex and
1027	      the localised-module complex of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}
1028	      transports the \(R\)-module exactness across an additive ladder, yielding
1029	      function exactness for the group-level coface differentials.
1030	  \end{itemize}
1031	\end{lemma}
1032	
1033	\begin{proof}
1034	  \uses{lem:section_cech_objd_apply, lem:section_cech_product_equiv,
1035	    lem:section_cech_coface_match, lem:section_cech_module_exact}
1036	  Fix \(p = q + 1 \geq 1\). By standard homological algebra it suffices to show that
1037	  the two consecutive coface differentials \(d^q\), \(d^{q+1}\) of the section
1038	  complex are exact as maps of abelian groups (exactness at a node iff vanishing
1039	  homology, via the short complex around it).
1040	
1041	  The function exactness is established by an additive ladder transport. The
1042	  vertical maps of the ladder are the degreewise additive isomorphisms
1043	  \(e_p\): the product equivalence of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
1044	  composed with the coordinatewise comparison \(\varphi_\sigma\) that identifies
1045	  each section group with the localised module \(M_{s_\sigma}\). The two
1046	  commuting squares of the ladder follow from
1047	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_objd_apply} (the differential unfolds as an
1048	  alternating sum of face restrictions) and Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}
1049	  (each face restriction equals the localisation coface), giving
1050	  \(e_{p+1} \circ d^p = d_M^{\,p} \circ e_p\). The horizontal exactness is
1051	  the \(R\)-module exactness of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact};
1052	  transporting it across the additive ladder yields function exactness for the
1053	  coface differentials of the section complex.
1054	\end{proof}

1056	\begin{definition}
1057	[The un-localised section {\v C}ech module complex \(D^\bullet\)]
1058	  \label{def:section_cech_module_complex}
1059	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.dCoeff,
1060	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.dCoface,

1066	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.dToCech_comm,

1068	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.cechCoface_apply,
1069	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.locDiff,

1073	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.fLoc,

1076	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.locDiff_fLoc,
1077	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.map_dDiff_eq_locDiff,
1078	    AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.spanIdx,

1080	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.comparison_isLocalizedModule,
1081	    AlgebraicGeometry.AwayComparison.Inverts.smul_pow_cancel}
1082	  \uses{def:standard_affine_cover, def:qcoh_sections_localized}
1083	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(M = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \mathcal{F})\)
1084	  the global sections of a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec}
1085	  R}\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\), and \(s : \iota \to R\) (\(\iota\) finite) a spanning
1086	  family of the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) =
1087	  \top\) (Definition~\ref{def:standard_affine_cover}). The \emph{un-localised section
1088	  {\v C}ech module complex} \(D^\bullet\) is the cochain complex of \(R\)-modules
1089	  whose degree-\(p\) term is the product over multi-indices
1090	  \(\sigma : \{0, \ldots, p\} \to \iota\) of the away localisations
1091	  \[
1092	    D^p \;=\; \prod_{\sigma} M_{s_\sigma},
1093	    \qquad s_\sigma = \prod_{k} s_{\sigma(k)},
1094	    \qquad M_{s_\sigma} = \operatorname{LocalizedModule}
1095	      \bigl(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers}(s_\sigma)\bigr)\, M,
1096	  \]
1097	  with differential \(d^p : D^p \to D^{p+1}\) the alternating sum
1098	  \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j\, \delta^j\) of the coface comparison maps \(\delta^j\), each of
1099	  which is the canonical away-localisation comparison
1100	  \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\) sending a fraction to its image under
1101	  the further localisation at the omitted factor. The single-coefficient comparison
1102	  map and its localisation property are recorded by the \(\mathtt{dCoface}\) and
1103	  \(\mathtt{dCoeff}\) data and the differential by \(\mathtt{dDiff}\) (with action
1104	  \(\mathtt{dDiff\_apply}\)); the auxiliary \(\mathtt{spanIdx}\)/\(\mathtt{spanIdx\_spec}\)
1105	  select a witnessing index of the spanning relation.
1106	
1107	  The complex \(D^\bullet\) is tied to the section {\v C}ech complex of
1108	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} and to its localisations by three
1109	  comparison families. First, the maps \(\mathtt{dToCech}\) identify each term
1110	  \(D^p\) with the degree-\(p\) section group
1111	  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) of
1112	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} as a localised module
1113	  (\(\mathtt{dToCech\_isLocalizedModule}\)), intertwining the differentials
1114	  (\(\mathtt{cechCoface\_dToCech}\), \(\mathtt{dToCech\_comm}\)); the section-side
1115	  cofaces are \(\mathtt{cechCofaceLin}\) with action \(\mathtt{cechCoface\_apply}\).
1116	  Second, localising \(D^\bullet\) at a spanning element \(s_r\) reproduces the
1117	  \emph{localised} {\v C}ech complex: the localised differential \(\mathtt{locDiff}\)
1118	  (action \(\mathtt{locDiff\_apply}\)) agrees, via
1119	  \(\mathtt{locDiff\_eq\_depDiff}\), with the dependent-coefficient combinatorial
1120	  differential, and its exactness is recorded as \(\mathtt{locDiff\_exact}\). The
1121	  localisation comparison maps \(\mathtt{fLoc}\) (action \(\mathtt{fLoc\_apply}\))
1122	  exhibit each localised term as a localised module
1123	  (\(\mathtt{fLoc\_isLocalizedModule}\)) intertwining \(\mathtt{dDiff}\) with
1124	  \(\mathtt{locDiff}\) (\(\mathtt{locDiff\_fLoc}\), \(\mathtt{map\_dDiff\_eq\_locDiff}\)).
1125	  The key algebraic fact underlying all three families is the localisation
1126	  transitivity \(M_a[1/b] = M_{ab}\): localising the away module \(M_{s_\sigma}\)
1127	  further at \(s_r\) gives \(M_{s_r s_\sigma}\). This is the keystone
1128	  \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.comparison\_isLocalizedModule}\) (the composite of two away
1129	  localisations is again the away localisation at the product), with the cancellation
1130	  bookkeeping \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.Inverts.smul\_pow\_cancel}\). Thus localising
1131	  \(D^\bullet\) at \(s_r\) (an \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.Away}\)) reproduces
1132	  the localised Čech complex of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}, and
1133	  exactness of \(D^\bullet\) can be checked one localisation at a time.
1134	\end{definition}

1136	\begin{lemma}
1137	
1138	\leanok
1139	[Positive-degree exactness of the section {\v C}ech module complex]
1140	  \label{lem:section_cech_module_exact}
1141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SectionCechModule.dDiff_exact}
1142	  \uses{def:section_cech_module_complex}
1143	  With the notation of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex}, the
1144	  un-localised section {\v C}ech module complex \(D^\bullet\) is exact in positive
1145	  degrees: for every \(p\),
1146	  \[
1147	    \operatorname{Function.Exact}\bigl(d^{p+1} : D^{p+1} \to D^{p+2},\;
1148	      d^{p+2} : D^{p+2} \to D^{p+3}\bigr).
1149	  \]
1150	  This is step~(a) of the affine vanishing: the node-by-node exactness of the
1151	  un-localised module complex, from which the homology vanishing of the section
1152	  {\v C}ech complex (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}, step~(c)) is read off.
1153	\end{lemma}
1154	
1155	\begin{proof}
1156	  \uses{def:section_cech_module_complex}
1157	  \leanok
1158	  Exactness of \(D^\bullet\) at each positive-degree node is a local-to-global
1159	  statement over the spanning family. By the local-to-global exactness criterion
1160	  \(\operatorname{exact\_of\_isLocalized\_span}(\operatorname{Set.range} s)\) (with the
1161	  spanning hypothesis \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\)), it
1162	  suffices to verify \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(d^{p+1}, d^{p+2})\) after
1163	  localising at each spanning element \(s_r\). In the localisation at \(s_r\) the
1164	  comparison family \(\mathtt{map\_dDiff\_eq\_locDiff}\) identifies the localised
1165	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) with the localised Čech differential \(\mathtt{locDiff}\)
1166	  of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} (using the localisation
1167	  transitivity \(M_a[1/b] = M_{ab}\) of
1168	  \(\mathtt{AwayComparison.comparison\_isLocalizedModule}\)). Exactness of that
1169	  localised differential is \(\mathtt{locDiff\_exact}\), which is in turn the
1170	  dependent-coefficient combinatorial exactness transported along
1171	  \(\mathtt{locDiff\_eq\_depDiff}\); the contracting homotopy at the fixed index
1172	  \(r\) (where \(s_r\) is invertible) furnishes \((dh + hd) = \operatorname{id}\),
1173	  giving the localised exactness one localisation at a time. Reassembling over the
1174	  spanning family yields the un-localised exactness.
1175	\end{proof}

4396	\begin{proof}
4397	  \uses{lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen}
4398	  The canonical homeomorphism \(\Spec R_g \xrightarrow{\sim} D(g)\) carries \(\Spec R_g\)
4399	  isomorphically onto \(D(g)\), so the first identity is just the image of the whole space under that
4400	  homeomorphism followed by the open inclusion. For the second, \(\bar f\) is the image of \(f\) under
4401	  \(R \to R_g\); its basic open \(D(\bar f) \subseteq \Spec R_g\) maps under the homeomorphism to
4402	  \(D(g) \cap D(f)\), since restricting to \(D(g)\) and then taking the locus where \(f\) is invertible
4403	  is the locus where both \(g\) and \(f\) are invertible. Finally \(D(g) \cap D(f) = D(gf)\) since the
4404	  basic open of a product is the intersection of the basic opens. These are equalities of opens, proved
4405	  set-theoretically; they are not definitional.
4406	\end{proof}

4408	\begin{lemma}[Natural section comparison between the tile and the ambient sheaf]
4409	  \label{lem:tile_section_comparison}
4410	  % NOTE: the \lean{} pin is deferred until the Lean decl is built; sync will reconcile it.
4411	  \uses{lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen, lem:restrict_obj_mathlib,
4412	    lem:tile_image_opens_identities}
4413	  In the situation of Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_image_opens_identities}, with
4414	  \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) the restriction of the quasi-coherent module
4415	  \(\mathcal{F}\) along \(\iota : \Spec R_g \cong D(g) \hookrightarrow X\), there is an \(R_g\)-linear
4416	  isomorphism, natural in the open \(V \subseteq \Spec R_g\) and intertwining the restriction maps of the
4417	  two sheaves,
4418	  \[
4419	    \Gamma_{R_g}\bigl(V, \mathcal{F}_{(g)}\bigr) \;\cong\; \Gamma_R\bigl(\iota(V), \mathcal{F}\bigr).
4420	  \]
4421	  % NOTE (Lean): Γ_{R_g}(−, F_(g)) is modulesSpecToSheaf.obj (ModuleCat R_g-valued);
4422	  % Γ_R(−, F) the corresponding functor for F over R.
4423	  Here \(\Gamma_{R_g}(-, \mathcal{F}_{(g)})\) is the global-ring section functor attached to the
4424	  presented tile over \(R_g\) (valued in \(R_g\)-modules), and \(\Gamma_R(-, \mathcal{F})\) the
4425	  corresponding functor for \(\mathcal{F}\) over \(R\).
4426	\end{lemma}
4427	\begin{proof}
4428	  \uses{lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen, lem:restrict_obj_mathlib,
4429	    lem:tile_image_opens_identities}
4430	  % NOTE (Lean): this is the load-bearing ~100--150 LOC construction.
4431	  % The local-ring functor is restrict_obj/restrict_map (defeq, Lemma lem:restrict_obj_mathlib).
4432	  % The global-ring functor is modulesSpecToSheaf.obj = forgetToSheafModuleCat composed with
4433	  % restrict-scalars along globalSectionsIso. The bridge is NOT rfl: the two section objects
4434	  % (modulesSpecToSheaf.obj F_(g)).presheaf.obj V and (modulesSpecToSheaf.obj F).presheaf.obj (ι V)
4435	  % differ by both the base ring and the provably-equal-but-not-rfl opens.
4436	  At the level of the local-ring section functor --- the sheaf-of-modules functor
4437	  \(\Gamma(\mathcal{M},-)\) valued over the stalk ring \(\Gamma(V,\mathcal{O})\) --- the equality
4438	  \(\Gamma(\mathcal{F}_{(g)},V) = \Gamma(\mathcal{F},\iota(V))\) holds definitionally
4439	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:restrict_obj_mathlib}). The content of the present lemma is to upgrade this
4440	  definitional equality from the local-ring functor \(\Gamma(\mathcal{M},-)\) to the global-ring
4441	  section functor \(\Gamma_{R_g}(-,\mathcal{F}_{(g)})\) (resp.\ \(\Gamma_R(-,\mathcal{F})\)). The
4442	  global-ring functor is obtained from the local-ring one by composing with restriction of scalars along
4443	  the global-sections identification \(R_g \cong \Gamma(\top,\mathcal{O}_{\Spec R_g})\)
4444	  (resp.\ \(R \cong \Gamma(\top,\mathcal{O}_X)\)). Concretely: take the definitional identification of
4445	  underlying additive groups, transport it across both global-sections restriction-of-scalars bookkeeping
4446	  maps to obtain an \(R_g\)-linear isomorphism, and verify naturality in \(V\) and compatibility with
4447	  restriction maps. The image opens that appear are identified by
4448	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_image_opens_identities}. This bridge is genuinely non-definitional: the two
4449	  global-ring section modules \(\Gamma_{R_g}(V,\mathcal{F}_{(g)})\) and
4450	  \(\Gamma_R(\iota(V),\mathcal{F})\) are not definitionally equal --- they differ both by the base
4451	  ring (\(R_g\) versus \(R\)) and by the provably-equal but not-definitionally-equal opens of
4452	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_image_opens_identities}.
4453	\end{proof}

4455	\begin{lemma}[Per-tile section localisation at \(f\)]
4456	  \label{lem:tile_section_localization}
4457	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tile_section_localization}
4458	  \uses{lem:qcoh_finite_presentation_cover, lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen,
4459	    lem:section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation, lem:restrict_obj_mathlib,
4460	    lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_restrictScalars_of_algebraMap,
4461	    lem:tile_image_opens_identities, lem:tile_section_comparison}
4462	  Let \(X = \Spec R\), let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, let \(f \in R\),
4463	  and let \(g \in R\) be either a cover element \(g = g_j\) or an overlap product \(g = g_j g_k\) of the
4464	  finite presentation cover of Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_finite_presentation_cover}, so that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) of
4465	  \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(\Spec R_g\) admits a global presentation. Then the restriction-of-sections map
4466	  \(\Gamma(D(g), \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\) exhibits its target as the localisation of
4467	  its source at the powers of \(f\); equivalently, there is a canonical \(R\)-linear isomorphism
4468	  \(\Gamma(D(g), \mathcal{F})_f \cong \Gamma(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\).
4469	\end{lemma}
4470	\begin{proof}
4471	  \uses{lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen, lem:section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation,
4472	    lem:restrict_obj_mathlib, lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_restrictScalars_of_algebraMap,
4473	    lem:tile_image_opens_identities, lem:tile_section_comparison}
4474	  % NOTE: the naive recipe "the section comparison is the restrict_obj rfl" is UNSOUND:
4475	  % restrict_obj is rfl only for the local-ring SheafOfModules functor Γ(M,-), whereas
4476	  % the localisation in this file lives in the global-ring functor (modulesSpecToSheaf.obj),
4477	  % which does NOT commute with restriction definitionally. Hence the five-step descent below.
4478	  Let \(\iota : \Spec R_g \xrightarrow{\sim} D(g) \hookrightarrow X\) be the open immersion of
4479	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_image_opens_identities}, with \(\bar f\) the image of \(f\) under the
4480	  localisation map \(R \to R_g\). The proof is a base-ring descent in five steps; the section
4481	  comparison it rests on is \emph{not} the definitional equality of
4482	  Lemma~\ref{lem:restrict_obj_mathlib} (see the proof of
4483	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_section_comparison}).
4484	
4485	  \emph{Step 1 (global presentation of the tile).} Since \(D(g)\) lies in a presentation member of the
4486	  cover, step B4 (Lemma~\ref{lem:presentation_modulesRestrictBasicOpen}) presents the tile
4487	  \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) globally over \(\Spec R_g\).
4488	
4489	  \emph{Step 2 (localisation over \(R_g\)).} Apply
4490	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_isLocalizedModule_of_presentation} to the globally presented
4491	  \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\) and \(\bar f\): the section-restriction map
4492	  \(\Gamma_{R_g}(\Spec R_g, \mathcal{F}_{(g)}) \to \Gamma_{R_g}(D(\bar f), \mathcal{F}_{(g)})\)
4493	  exhibits its target as a localisation at the powers of \(\bar f\), \emph{as \(R_g\)-modules}. Note this
4494	  is over \(R_g\), not yet over \(R\).
4495	
4496	  \emph{Step 3 (opens identities).} By Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_image_opens_identities} the two image opens of
4497	  \(\iota\) are \(\iota(\Spec R_g) = D(g)\) and \(\iota(D(\bar f)) = D(gf) = D(g) \cap D(f)\).
4498	
4499	  \emph{Step 4 (the section comparison).} By Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_section_comparison} there is an
4500	  \(R_g\)-linear isomorphism, natural in \(V\) and intertwining restriction maps,
4501	  \(\Gamma_{R_g}(V, \mathcal{F}_{(g)}) \cong \Gamma_R(\iota(V), \mathcal{F})\). This is the load-bearing
4502	  step: it bridges the global-ring section functor (where Step 2's localisation lives) and the ambient
4503	  sheaf \(\mathcal{F}\) over \(R\); it is a genuine natural-isomorphism construction, not a definitional
4504	  identification. Transporting Step 2's localisation-at-\(\bar f\) property across this isomorphism
4505	  (\(R_g\)-linearly) and matching source and target opens by Step 3 yields: the map
4506	  \(\Gamma_R(D(g), \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma_R(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\) exhibits its target as a
4507	  localisation at the powers of \(\bar f\), \emph{still as \(R_g\)-modules}.
4508	
4509	  \emph{Step 5 (base-ring descent to \(R\)).} Finally descend the base ring from \(R_g\) to \(R\) by
4510	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isLocalizedModule_powers_restrictScalars_of_algebraMap}, applied with \(A = R_g\) and the
4511	  scalar tower \(R \to R_g\) on the two section modules: since \(\bar f\) is the image of \(f\) under
4512	  \(R \to R_g\), the powers of \(\bar f\) descend to the powers of \(f\), and the \(R\)-linear
4513	  restriction of the Step-4 map exhibits \(\Gamma_R(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\) as the localisation of
4514	  \(\Gamma_R(D(g), \mathcal{F})\) at the powers of \(f\) \emph{over \(R\)}. Equivalently there is a
4515	  canonical \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(\Gamma(D(g), \mathcal{F})_f \cong \Gamma(D(gf), \mathcal{F})\).
4516	
4517	  Crucially this localisation is carried out entirely on the tile \(\mathcal{F}_{(g)}\), where
4518	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is associated to a module (the tile is globally presented, hence of the form
4519	  \(\widetilde{M_g}\)), and never on
4520	  the global object \(\mathcal{F}\). This is exactly the structural feature that keeps the keystone
4521	  Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule} non-circular: the keystone-at-\(g\) statement is
4522	  \emph{not} invoked; only the presented-tile lemma is.
4523	\end{proof}

4525	\begin{lemma}[Kernel comparison: the localised global equalizer matches the \(D(f)\)-cover equalizer]
4526	  \label{lem:qcoh_section_kernel_comparison}
4527	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.qcoh_section_kernel_comparison}
4528	  \uses{lem:qcoh_finite_presentation_cover, lem:qcoh_section_equalizer,
4529	    lem:localized_module_map_exact_mathlib, lem:tile_section_localization}
4530	  Let \(X = \Spec R\), let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, let \(f \in R\),
4531	  and let \(g_1, \ldots, g_n\) be the finite presentation cover of
4532	  Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_finite_presentation_cover}, with \(\operatorname{span}\{g_j\} = R\). Then the
4533	  canonical \(R\)-linear map \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f \to \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) induced by
4534	  \(\rho_f : \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) (the localisation lift of \(\rho_f\)
4535	  at the powers of \(f\)) is an isomorphism.
4536	\end{lemma}
4537	\begin{proof}
4538	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_equalizer, lem:localized_module_map_exact_mathlib,
4539	    lem:tile_section_localization}
4540	  Write the degree-\(0/1\) equalizer (Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_section_equalizer}) for \(\mathcal{F}\) on the
4541	  standard cover \(X = \bigcup_j D(g_j)\) (take \(W = X\)):
4542	  \[
4543	    0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \xrightarrow{\rho} \prod_j \Gamma(D(g_j), \mathcal{F})
4544	      \xrightarrow{\delta} \prod_{j,k} \Gamma(D(g_j g_k), \mathcal{F}),
4545	  \]
4546	  and the same equalizer for the induced cover \(D(f) = \bigcup_j \bigl(D(f) \cap D(g_j)\bigr)\) of
4547	  \(D(f)\) (take \(W = D(f)\), using \(D(f) \cap D(g_j) = D(g_j f)\)):
4548	  \[
4549	    0 \to \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F}) \xrightarrow{\rho'} \prod_j \Gamma(D(g_j f), \mathcal{F})
4550	      \xrightarrow{\delta'} \prod_{j,k} \Gamma(D(g_j g_k f), \mathcal{F}).
4551	  \]
4552	  Localise the first sequence at the powers of \(f\). Localisation is exact
4553	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:localized_module_map_exact_mathlib}) and commutes with the finite products, so
4554	  \[
4555	    0 \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f \xrightarrow{\rho_f} \prod_j \Gamma(D(g_j), \mathcal{F})_f
4556	      \xrightarrow{\delta_f} \prod_{j,k} \Gamma(D(g_j g_k), \mathcal{F})_f
4557	  \]
4558	  is exact; it exhibits \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f\) as the kernel of \(\delta_f\) (and \(\rho_f\) here
4559	  is the localisation lift of the restriction product, hence the localisation lift of \(\rho_f\) on its
4560	  first component).
4561	
4562	  For every cover element \(g_j\) and overlap product \(g_j g_k\), the per-tile localisation
4563	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:tile_section_localization}) supplies canonical \(R\)-linear isomorphisms
4564	  \[
4565	    \Gamma(D(g_j), \mathcal{F})_f \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \Gamma(D(g_j f), \mathcal{F}),
4566	    \qquad
4567	    \Gamma(D(g_j g_k), \mathcal{F})_f \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \Gamma(D(g_j g_k f), \mathcal{F}),
4568	  \]
4569	  hence isomorphisms of the product terms
4570	  \(\prod_j \Gamma(D(g_j), \mathcal{F})_f \cong \prod_j \Gamma(D(g_j f), \mathcal{F})\) and
4571	  \(\prod_{j,k} \Gamma(D(g_j g_k), \mathcal{F})_f \cong \prod_{j,k} \Gamma(D(g_j g_k f), \mathcal{F})\).
4572	  These isomorphisms intertwine the overlap differentials,
4573	  \(\delta' \circ (\text{iso}) = (\text{iso}) \circ \delta_f\): both composites carry a localised cover
4574	  section to its restriction on the overlap tile, and the two routes agree because restriction along the
4575	  inclusion \(D(g_j g_k f) \subseteq D(g_j g_k)\) and localisation at \(f\) are induced by one and the
4576	  same ring map. This is the degree-\(0/1\) naturality of restriction-versus-localisation. The same
4577	  square commutes for the first maps: under the cover-term identification \(\rho' = (\text{iso}) \circ
4578	  \rho_f\), because restricting a global section to \(D(g_j)\) and then localising at \(f\) equals first
4579	  applying \(\rho_f\) and then restricting to \(D(g_j f)\).
4580	
4581	  Thus the two left-exact sequences are isomorphic from the cover term onward by isomorphisms commuting
4582	  with the differentials. Passing to kernels, the induced map
4583	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f \cong \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) is an isomorphism, and by the commuting
4584	  first square it is precisely the canonical map induced by \(\rho_f\). Hence that map is an
4585	  isomorphism.
4586	\end{proof}

6235	\section{The {\v C}ech complex computes $R^i f_*$}

6237	The comparison is proved by the \emph{acyclic-resolution} (Cartan--Leray) route. The
6238	augmented {\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) whose terms are
6239	acyclic for \(f_*\); the abstract homological-algebra
6240	Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then identifies the derived
6241	functor with the cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\). The two
6242	geometric inputs are isolated in the following sub-lemmas.

6244	\begin{lemma}

6270	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with all
6271	  intersections affine. Form the augmented {\v C}ech complex on \(X\),

6287	  Exactness of a complex of sheaves is a local question on \(X\), so it may be
6288	  checked on the stalks, equivalently over each affine open of a basis. Restricting
6289	  to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) on which \(\mathcal{F}|_U =
6290	  \widetilde{M}\) and over which the cover restricts to a standard cover \(U =
6291	  \bigcup D(f_i)\), the augmented {\v C}ech complex becomes the extended complex of
6292	  localisations
6293	  \(0 \to M \to \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to
6294	  \cdots\). Its exactness is exactly the standard-cover vanishing of
6295	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}, witnessed by the prime-local contracting
6296	  homotopy \(h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}\). As this
6297	  holds over every affine of the basis, the augmented complex is exact on \(X\).
6298	\end{proof}

6300	\begin{lemma}
6301	[Cohomology sheaf is the sheafification of the objectwise homology]
6302	  \label{def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology}
6303	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.homologyIsoSheafify,
6304	    PresheafOfModules.counitComplexIso,
6305	    PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdditive,
6306	    CategoryTheory.Functor.mapHomologyIso'}
6307	  % NOTE: project-built generalization. Stacks Tag~01XJ
6308	  % (\texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}) establishes the consequence
6309	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F} \cong \text{sheafification of }(V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V),\mathcal{F}))$
6310	  % but does not state sheafification commuting with the homology of an arbitrary
6311	  % cochain complex as a standalone lemma. No verbatim Stacks source quote for this block.
6312	  Let \(K\) be a cochain complex of presheaves of modules over a site admitting
6313	  sheafification. For every index \(i\) there is a natural
6314	  isomorphism between the \(i\)-th homology of the sheafified complex and the
6315	  sheafification of the \(i\)-th objectwise (presheaf-level) homology of \(K\),
6316	  \[
6317	    H^i\bigl(\operatorname{sheafify}(K)\bigr)
6318	      \;\cong\;
6319	    \operatorname{sheafify}\bigl(H^i(K)\bigr).
6320	  \]
6321	  Equivalently, sheafification commutes with the formation of homology. This is the
6322	  reusable engine underlying the presheaf description of the higher direct images
6323	  (Stacks Tag~01XJ): it is what turns the resolution-internal cohomology sheaf into a
6324	  sheafification of an explicit cohomology presheaf.
6325	\end{lemma}
6326	
6327	\begin{proof}
6328	  Sheafification is exact: as the left adjoint of the inclusion of sheaves into
6329	  presheaves it preserves all colimits, and on a site admitting sheafification it
6330	  additionally preserves finite limits, so it preserves both the kernels and the
6331	  cokernels through which homology is computed. An exact additive functor commutes with
6332	  the homology functor of a cochain complex; chasing this through the counit complex
6333	  isomorphism \(\operatorname{sheafify}(\iota K) \cong K\) (the inclusion–sheafification
6334	  adjunction counit, an isomorphism on sheaves) gives
6335	  \(H^i(K) \cong H^i(\operatorname{sheafify} K) \cong
6336	  \operatorname{sheafify}(H^i(K))\). The supporting bricks are the
6337	  additivity of sheafification, the counit complex isomorphism, and the
6338	  complex-level transport of the short-complex map–homology isomorphism along an
6339	  additive homology-preserving functor.
6340	\end{proof}

6342	\begin{lemma}
6343	[Presheaf description of the higher direct images]
6344	  \label{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}
6345	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_iso_sheafify_presheafHomology,
6346	    AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardResolutionPresheafComplex}
6347	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology}
6348	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology, Tag 01XJ, Lemma
6349	  % \texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images} (the higher direct images are the
6350	  % sheafifications of the cohomology presheaves of preimages) (read from
6351	  % references/stacks-cohomology.tex, L591--603).
6352	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to Y$ be a morphism of ringed spaces.
6353	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
6354	  % The sheaves $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ are the sheaves
6355	  % associated to the presheaves
6356	  % $$
6357	  % V \longmapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F})
6358	  % $$
6359	  % with restriction mappings as in Equation (\ref{equation-restriction-mapping}).
6360	  % There is a similar statement for $R^if_*$ applied to a
6361	  % bounded below complex $\mathcal{F}^\bullet$."
6362	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology, Tag 01XJ
6363	  (\texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}).}
6364	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{G}\) an
6365	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and assume the category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has
6366	  enough injective objects, so that the derived pushforward \(R^k f_*\) is defined.
6367	  Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
6368	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and push it forward degreewise to obtain the cochain complex
6369	  of presheaves of modules \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) on \(S\). For every \(k \geq 0\)
6370	  the higher direct image \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) is isomorphic to the
6371	  \emph{sheafification of the presheaf of objectwise homology} of this pushed complex,
6372	  \[
6373	    R^k f_*\mathcal{G}
6374	      \;\cong\;
6375	    \operatorname{sheafify}\Bigl(\,V \longmapsto
6376	      H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)\,\Bigr),
6377	    \qquad V \subseteq S \text{ open},
6378	  \]
6379	  the sheafification of the \(k\)-th presheaf-level homology of
6380	  \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\). As a closing remark, this gives the affine-local
6381	  vanishing criterion consumed downstream
6382	  (Lemmas~\ref{lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp} and
6383	  \ref{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic}): since the sheafification of a presheaf
6384	  vanishes exactly when that presheaf vanishes on a basis of opens,
6385	  \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G} = 0\) iff \(H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr) = 0\)
6386	  for every \(V\) in a basis of the topology of \(S\); the affine opens form such a
6387	  basis, so it suffices to check the vanishing on affine \(V\).
6388	
6389	  The identification of the displayed presheaf homology with the absolute cohomology
6390	  of the preimage,
6391	  \[
6392	    H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)
6393	      \;=\;
6394	    H^k\bigl(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
6395	  \]
6396	  which uses that \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is again injective over
6397	  \(f^{-1}(V)\), is supplied at point of use; this lemma records the
6398	  resolution-internal sheafify-of-objectwise-homology form.
6399	\end{lemma}
6400	
6401	\begin{proof}
6402	  \uses{def:higher_direct_image, def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology}
6403	  Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{G} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
6404	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. By definition \(R^k f_*\mathcal{G}\) is the \(k\)-th
6405	  cohomology sheaf of the complex \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) of presheaves of modules
6406	  over \(S\). The comparison engine of
6407	  Lemma~\ref{def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology} now applies: taking homology in
6408	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules is the sheafification of the objectwise (presheaf-level)
6409	  homology, because the sheafification/inclusion adjunction is exact (it preserves the
6410	  finite limits and colimits computing kernels and cokernels). Hence
6411	  \[
6412	    R^k f_*\mathcal{G}
6413	      \;\cong\;
6414	    \operatorname{sheafify}\Bigl(\,V \longmapsto
6415	      H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)\,\Bigr),
6416	  \]
6417	  which is the stated isomorphism. Since the sheafification of a presheaf vanishes
6418	  exactly when the underlying presheaf vanishes on a basis of opens, this yields the
6419	  affine-local vanishing criterion.
6420	\end{proof}

6422	\begin{lemma}
6423	[Affine open immersions are $(\cdot)_*$-acyclic and degenerate the composition]
6424	  \label{lem:open_immersion_pushforward_comp}
6425	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_openImmersion_comp}
6426	  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}
6427	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
6428	  % \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing} (relative vanishing of higher direct
6429	  % images for affine morphisms) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
6430	  % L180--199).
6431	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
6432	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
6433	  % If $f$ is affine then $R^if_*\mathcal{F} = 0$ for all $i > 0$."
6434	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
6435	  \texttt{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}.}
6436	  Let \(X\) be separated, \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\) the open immersion of an affine
6437	  open \(U\), and \(f : X \to S\) any morphism, with composite \(g := f \circ j : U
6438	  \to S\). Then:
6439	  \begin{enumerate}
6440	    \item \(j\) is an affine morphism (an open immersion of an affine open into a
6441	      separated scheme), so its higher direct images vanish: \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H} = 0\)
6442	      for all \(q \geq 1\) and every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-module
6443	      \(\mathcal{H}\); and
6444	    \item consequently, for every quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{H}\) on \(U\) and every
6445	      \(k \geq 0\),
6446	      \[
6447	        R^k f_*\bigl(j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr) \;\cong\; R^k g_*\mathcal{H}.
6448	      \]
6449	  \end{enumerate}
6450	\end{lemma}
6451	
6452	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "According to
6453	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
6454	% the sheaf
6455	% $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
6456	% $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$.
6457	% By assumption, whenever $V$ is affine we have that $f^{-1}(V)$ is
6458	% affine, see Morphisms, Definition \ref{morphisms-definition-affine}.
6459	% By Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero} we conclude that
6460	% $H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}) = 0$
6461	% whenever $V$ is affine. Since $S$ has a basis consisting of affine
6462	% opens we win." (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L187--198)
6463	\begin{proof}
6464	  \uses{lem:affine_serre_vanishing, lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf, lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}
6465	  (1) An open immersion of an affine open \(U\) into a separated scheme \(X\) is an
6466	  affine morphism: separatedness makes the immersion \(j\) quasi-separated and the
6467	  preimage of any affine open of \(X\) meets \(U\) in an affine, so \(j\) is affine.
6468	  The relative affine vanishing for affine morphisms then gives \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H} =
6469	  0\) for \(q \geq 1\); concretely, by the presheaf description
6470	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}) \(R^q j_*\mathcal{H}\) is the
6471	  sheafification of \(V \mapsto H^q(j^{-1}(V), \mathcal{H})\), and for affine \(V\) the
6472	  preimage \(j^{-1}(V)\) is affine, on which the Serre vanishing of
6473	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} kills \(H^q\) for \(q \geq 1\).
6474	
6475	  (2) Choose an injective resolution \(\mathcal{H} \to \mathcal{I}^\bullet\) in
6476	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules and apply \(j_*\) degreewise. Since each \(\mathcal{I}^n\)
6477	  is \(j_*\)-acyclic by part (1) (\(R^q j_*\mathcal{I}^n = 0\) for \(q \geq 1\)), the
6478	  complex \(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(j_*\mathcal{H}\).
6479	
6480	  Each \(j_*\mathcal{I}^n\) is \(f_*\)-acyclic. By the presheaf description
6481	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}), \(R^k f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^n)\) is the
6482	  sheafification of
6483	  \[
6484	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^k\bigl(f^{-1}(V), (j_*\mathcal{I}^n)|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr)
6485	      \;=\; H^k\bigl(U \cap f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
6486	  \]
6487	  the equality because \((j_*\mathcal{I}^n)|_{f^{-1}(V)}\) is the pushforward of
6488	  \(\mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}\) along the open immersion
6489	  \(U \cap f^{-1}(V) \hookrightarrow f^{-1}(V)\), which leaves cohomology unchanged. For
6490	  \(V = \operatorname{Spec}(A) \subseteq S\) affine, \(f^{-1}(V)\) is quasi-compact and,
6491	  \(X\) being separated and \(j\) affine, the affine open \(U\) meets it in the affine
6492	  open \(U \cap f^{-1}(V)\); hence by Serre vanishing
6493	  Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing} the cohomology
6494	  \(H^k(U \cap f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{I}^n|_{U \cap f^{-1}(V)}) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\).
6495	  Since the affine opens form a basis of \(S\), this forces
6496	  \(R^k f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^n) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\).
6497	
6498	  Thus \(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is an \(f_*\)-acyclic resolution of
6499	  \(j_*\mathcal{H}\), and the acyclic-resolution comparison
6500	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} applied with \(G = f_*\) gives,
6501	  for every \(k\),
6502	  \[
6503	    R^k f_*\bigl(j_*\mathcal{H}\bigr)
6504	      \;\cong\; H^k\bigl(f_*(j_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)\bigr)
6505	      \;=\; H^k\bigl((f \circ j)_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\bigr)
6506	      \;=\; R^k g_*\mathcal{H},
6507	  \]
6508	  the middle equality because \(f_* \circ j_* = (f \circ j)_* = g_*\), and the last
6509	  because \(\mathcal{I}^\bullet\) is an injective resolution of \(\mathcal{H}\)
6510	  computing \(R^k g_*\mathcal{H}\).
6511	\end{proof}
